input.prompt:
I'm building a photo viewer page in my .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10. I want
the image to fill the entire screen edge-to-edge, going behind the status bar
and navigation bar. But my image still has padding around it even though I set
SafeAreaEdges="None" on the ContentPage. What am I missing?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a photo viewer page in my .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10. I want
the image to fill the entire screen edge-to-edge, going behind the status bar
and navigation bar. But my image still has padding around it even though I set
SafeAreaEdges="None" on the ContentPage. What am I missing?

data.content:
`SafeAreaEdges="None"` only tells MAUI not to add **padding** for the safe area insets — it doesn't make the system bars transparent or force your content behind them. You likely need two more things:

**1. Hide any app-level navigation chrome**

If you're using `Shell` or `NavigationPage`, their toolbar still takes space:

```xml
<!-- Shell -->
<ContentPage Shell.NavBarIsVisible="False"
             Shell.TabBarIsVisible="False"
             SafeAreaEdges="None">

<!-- Or NavigationPage -->
<ContentPage NavigationPage.HasNavigationBar="False"
             SafeAreaEdges="None">
```

**2. Enable edge-to-edge + transparent system bars (Android)**

On Android 15+ edge-to-edge is automatic, but the system bars may still have a scrim. For older Android versions, you must opt in. In your `MainActivity.cs` or page handler:

```csharp
#if ANDROID
using AndroidX.Core.View;
using Microsoft.Maui.Controls.Platform.Compatibility;

// In your MainActivity or a custom handler:
protected override void OnCreate(Bundle? savedInstanceState)
{
    base.OnCreate(savedInstanceState);

    // Draw behind system bars on pre-Android 15
    WindowCompat.SetDecorFitsSystemWindows(Window!, false);

    // Make system bars fully transparent
    Window!.SetStatusBarColor(Android.Graphics.Color.Transparent);
    Window!.SetNavigationBarColor(Android.Graphics.Color.Transparent);
}
#endif
```

**3. Ensure the Image fills the page**

```xml
<ContentPage ...
             SafeAreaEdges="None"
             Padding="0">
    <Image Source="photo.jpg"
           Aspect="AspectFill"
           VerticalOptions="Fill"
           HorizontalOptions="Fill" />
</ContentPage>
```

**Summary of what each piece does:**

| Setting | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `SafeAreaEdges="None"` | Don't pad content away from notch/bars |
| `Shell.NavBarIsVisible="False"` | Remove MAUI's own navigation bar |
| `SetDecorFitsSystemWindows(false)` | Let content draw behind Android system bars |
| `SetStatusBarColor(Transparent)` | Remove status bar background |
| `SetNavigationBarColor(Transparent)` | Remove nav bar background |

On **iOS**, `SafeAreaEdges="None"` plus hiding the nav bar is usually sufficient — the content will extend behind the notch and home indicator automatically.
data.reasoningText:
The user is working on a .NET MAUI app targeting .NET 10 and wants an image to go edge-to-edge (behind status bar and navigation bar), but setting `SafeAreaEdges="None"` on the ContentPage isn't working. Let me think about what's needed here.

In .NET MAUI, to achieve edge-to-edge content that goes behind system bars, there are a few things to consider:

1. **Safe area handling** - `SafeAreaEdges` controls which edges respect the safe area insets. Setting it to `None` should tell the page not to pad for safe areas.


2. **Edge-to-edge display** - Starting with .NET 9+, MAUI has proper edge-to-edge support, and on Android 15+ it's enabled by default, but older versions may need explicit configuration to draw content behind system bars.
3. The real issue is likely that `SafeAreaEdges` alone might not be enough—the app needs to actually be configured to render edge-to-edge at the platform level, which involves more than just the XAML property.

For .NET 10, the approach differs by platform: on Android, I'd need to handle transparent status and navigation bars, potentially using `WindowCompat.SetDecorFitsSystemWindows(false)` for older API levels, while iOS just needs `SafeAreaEdges="None"`. The user probably needs to combine the XAML setting with platform-specific configuration to fully achieve the edge-to-edge effect. The status bar background also needs to be transparent, and I should check if there's a `Shell` or `NavigationPage` adding extra chrome—if so, I'd need to hide those navigation elements. For truly edge-to-edge rendering on Android in .NET 9+, beyond setting `SafeAreaEdges="None"`, I may need to use platform-specific code to make system bars transparent and ensure the app is in edge-to-edge mode on older Android versions.
